{"step_by_step_thinking": "The query asks whether bare-metal stent (BMS) implantation is still justifiable in high bleeding risk patients undergoing percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI). Multiple documents support that BMS use has declined due to the superiority of drug-eluting stents (DES) in reducing restenosis and improving long-term outcomes. However, several sources explicitly state that BMS may still be indicated in high bleeding risk patients. For instance, Document [0] states that BMS may be reasonable in patients with active bleeding or very high bleeding risk on dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT). Document [1] notes that BMS are indicated in patients with limited DAPT options, including those with bleeding risk or poor compliance. Document [3] and [17] reinforce that in high bleeding risk patients, or those unable to tolerate prolonged DAPT (e.g., due to surgery within 4-6 weeks or compliance issues), BMS are preferred. Document [20] explicitly states that BMS are preferred when a patient cannot take longer duration of DAPT. These findings indicate that despite the general shift toward DES, BMS remain a justified option in high bleeding risk scenarios. Therefore, the evidence supports that BMS implantation is still justifiable in such patients.", "answer_choice": "A"}
